content.text: The context window of a language model is the maximum number of tokens the model can attend to in a single forward pass. It defines a hard boundary: text beyond this limit is invisible to the model. Everything within the context window — including the system prompt, conversation history, retrieved documents, tool outputs, and the current user message — must fit within this budget simultaneously, because the transformer's self-attention mechanism relates every position to every other position within the window.

Tokens are the atomic units that fill the context window. Each token represents a subword, word fragment, character, or byte sequence as determined by the tokenizer. The string 'The context window determines...' is not one unit in the model's view; it is a sequence of tokens such as ['The', ' context', ' window', ' determines', '...']. The model allocates one vector of dimension
to each token position and one row of attention keys, queries, and values per position per attention head.

Context windows have grown dramatically over recent model generations: GPT-3 had 4,096 tokens; GPT-4-turbo extended to 128,000; Gemini 1.5 Pro reached 1,000,000. Each doubling of context introduces significant engineering challenges because self-attention is quadratic in sequence length — attending over 128K tokens requires approximately 1,024 times more computation per layer than attending over 4K tokens.

The context window is the model's working memory. Everything the model knows about the current task must either be stored in its weights (as learned world knowledge) or placed explicitly in the context window. Tasks that require reasoning over long documents, multi-turn conversations, codebases, or retrieved search results are all bounded by this limit.

Exceeding the context window produces silent failures. Most inference APIs silently truncate input that exceeds the context limit, discarding the oldest or least-privileged tokens. A prompt engineering error that places the most important instructions near the beginning of a truncated document may cause those instructions to be silently removed. Understanding and managing the context budget is therefore a core reliability concern in agentic AI systems.

For cost and latency, the context window is also critical. Most commercial APIs price by input token count: every token in the context — including the system prompt, conversation history, and retrieved chunks — is billed on every call. A 10,000-token system prompt multiplied by 1,000 daily conversations costs 10 million input tokens per day, which at GPT-4-turbo pricing (~$0.01/1K tokens) is $100/day just in system prompt cost.
Large context windows allow multi-step reasoning, long chain-of-thought traces, and complex problem decomposition entirely within a single inference call.
Tasks like legal contract review, research paper summarization, and codebase-level refactoring become feasible when entire documents fit in context.
Agentic systems can maintain longer conversation histories, reducing the need for lossy compression of prior turns.
RAG pipelines can retrieve and pass more candidate chunks simultaneously, improving recall without requiring separate re-ranking passes.
Larger windows support more few-shot examples, enabling sophisticated in-context learning that rivals fine-tuning for specialized tasks.
. Doubling the context doubles memory but quadruples FLOPs per layer.

Empirically, transformer models attend most strongly to tokens near the beginning and end of the context window. Information in the middle of a very long context is often underweighted. This 'lost in the middle' phenomenon means that longer contexts do not simply provide more information — they can dilute relevant information.
Commercial API pricing is linear in token count, making long-context calls significantly more expensive. A 128K-token call costs 32x more than a 4K-token call at the same per-token rate.

Longer contexts increase time-to-first-token (TTFT) because the prefill phase (computing keys and values for all input tokens) scales with context length. For latency-sensitive applications, context management is essential.
The key-value cache grows linearly with context length. A 128K-context GPT-4 call requires approximately 32 GB of KV cache memory, which constrains batch sizes in self-hosted deployments.
Every transformer layer applies scaled dot-product attention over all token positions within the context window. Each query vector at position
is compared against all key vectors at positions

The model uses positional encodings — either fixed sinusoidal vectors (original Transformer), learned absolute positions (BERT, GPT-2), or rotary position embeddings (RoPE, used in LLaMA, Mistral) — to distinguish positions within the window. The context limit is therefore set by the maximum position index that the positional encoding scheme is trained to handle.

During autoregressive generation, the model generates one token at a time. Without optimization, this would require recomputing attention over the entire context for each new token — O(n) forward passes each of O(n²) cost = O(n³) total. The KV cache avoids this: after the prefill phase, the key and value tensors for all existing context tokens are cached in GPU memory. Each new token only computes its own query and looks up the cached keys and values. The per-token generation cost drops to O(n) in context length.

Techniques for extending context beyond the training limit include: Rotary Position Interpolation (RoPE scaling), Alibi (attention linear biases that extrapolate naturally), Longformer sparse attention (local windows + global tokens), Ring Attention (distributing the context across multiple GPUs), and flash-attention optimized kernels that reduce memory from O(n²) to O(n) via tiling.
The diagram below illustrates how token budget is allocated across prompt components, and how a sliding window strategy handles documents that exceed the context limit.

attrs.alt: Context window token budget and sliding window strategy

content.text: The token sequence fills from position 0 to N-1. The system prompt occupies the earliest positions, followed by conversation history, the current user turn, retrieved context (for RAG), and finally the reserved output space. Because APIs bill input tokens and the model must output within remaining space, a practical engineering convention is to subtract an output reserve (typically 1,000–4,000 tokens) from the maximum context before computing how much input can fit.
Use maximum-context models when processing full legal contracts, long research papers, or large codebases where truncation would lose critical information.

Use context management strategies (chunking, summarization, hierarchical retrieval) when your data volume consistently exceeds even large context windows, or when cost and latency constraints make full-context calls impractical.
Use a sliding window approach for sequential documents where local context is sufficient for each chunk (e.g., sequential translation, section-by-section summarization).
Prefer smaller context fills for latency-sensitive applications: a 4K context call has dramatically lower TTFT than a 128K context call, even for a model that supports both.
Use explicit token counting (via tiktoken or the model's tokenizer) before sending any prompt to avoid silent truncation. Never estimate token count from character count.

import tiktoken
from typing import Optional

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

class ContextWindowManager:
    """Manage a token budget for an LLM context window."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        max_context_tokens: int = 128_000,
        output_reserve: int = 2_000,
        encoding_name: str = "cl100k_base",
    ):
        self.max_context = max_context_tokens
        self.output_reserve = output_reserve
        self.enc = tiktoken.get_encoding(encoding_name)
        self._usable = max_context_tokens - output_reserve

    def count(self, text: str) -> int:
        return len(self.enc.encode(text))

    def count_messages(self, messages: list[dict]) -> int:
        """Count tokens for a list of chat messages (OpenAI format)."""
        total = 0
        for msg in messages:
            # Each message has role overhead (~4 tokens) plus content
            total += 4 + self.count(msg.get("content", ""))
        total += 3  # priming for assistant reply
        return total

    def fits(self, messages: list[dict], extra_text: str = "") -> bool:
        return (
            self.count_messages(messages) + self.count(extra_text)
            <= self._usable
        )

    def truncate_history(
        self,
        system_msg: dict,
        history: list[dict],
        new_msg: dict,
    ) -> list[dict]:
        """Trim oldest history messages until everything fits."""
        messages = [system_msg] + history + [new_msg]
        while not self.fits(messages) and len(history) > 0:
            history = history[2:]  # drop oldest user+assistant pair
            messages = [system_msg] + history + [new_msg]
        return messages

# Usage
mgr = ContextWindowManager(max_context_tokens=16_000, output_reserve=1_000)

system = {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."}
history = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "What is Python?"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "Python is a high-level programming language..."},
] * 20  # simulate long history

new_msg = {"role": "user", "content": "What is a context window?"}
trimmed = mgr.truncate_history(system, history, new_msg)
print(f"Original history: {len(history)} messages")
print(f"Trimmed history: {len(trimmed) - 2} messages")  # minus system + new
print(f"Token count: {mgr.count_messages(trimmed)}")

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

def chunk_text(
    text: str,
    max_tokens: int = 3_000,
    overlap_tokens: int = 200,
) -> list[str]:
    """Split text into overlapping token-bounded chunks."""
    tokens = enc.encode(text)
    chunks = []
    start = 0
    while start < len(tokens):
        end = min(start + max_tokens, len(tokens))
        chunk_ids = tokens[start:end]
        chunks.append(enc.decode(chunk_ids))
        if end == len(tokens):
            break
        # Move start forward by (max_tokens - overlap), not max_tokens
        start = end - overlap_tokens
    return chunks

# Process a large document
long_doc = "This is a sentence. " * 2000  # ~14,000 tokens

chunks = chunk_text(long_doc, max_tokens=3_000, overlap_tokens=200)
print(f"Document: {len(enc.encode(long_doc)):,} tokens")
print(f"Chunks: {len(chunks)}")
for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
    n = len(enc.encode(chunk))
    print(f"  Chunk {i}: {n} tokens")
Token Budget Tracking for Agentic Pipelines

import tiktoken
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

@dataclass
class TokenBudget:
    """Track token consumption across agentic steps."""
    max_tokens: int = 128_000
    output_reserve: int = 2_000
    _log: list[tuple[str, int]] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def consumed(self) -> int:
        return sum(n for _, n in self._log)

    @property
    def remaining(self) -> int:
        return self.max_tokens - self.output_reserve - self.consumed

    def add(self, label: str, text: str) -> int:
        n = len(enc.encode(text))
        self._log.append((label, n))
        return n

    def report(self) -> None:
        print(f"\nToken Budget Report ({self.max_tokens:,} total):")
        for label, n in self._log:
            print(f"  {label:<30} {n:>8,} tokens")
        print(f"  {'Consumed':<30} {self.consumed:>8,} tokens")
        print(f"  {'Reserved (output)':<30} {self.output_reserve:>8,} tokens")
        print(f"  {'Remaining':<30} {self.remaining:>8,} tokens")

# Simulate an agentic pipeline
budget = TokenBudget(max_tokens=16_000, output_reserve=2_000)

budget.add("System prompt", "You are a research assistant...")
budget.add("Tool definitions", "search(q: str) -> list[str]...")
budget.add("User question", "Summarize the key findings in the attached document.")
budget.add("Retrieved doc chunk 1", "According to the study... " * 100)
budget.add("Retrieved doc chunk 2", "In conclusion... " * 50)
budget.add("Tool call result", '{"status": "ok", "results": [...]}')

budget.report()

if budget.remaining < 500:
    print("WARNING: Low context budget — consider compressing history")
In production RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) systems, context window management determines retrieval strategy. A typical pattern for a 128K-context model serving customer support queries:

import tiktoken
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a customer support agent for Acme Corp.
Answer questions using only the provided documentation excerpts.
If the answer is not in the excerpts, say "I don't have that information." """

MAX_CONTEXT = 128_000
OUTPUT_RESERVE = 4_000
SYSTEM_TOKENS = len(enc.encode(SYSTEM_PROMPT))

def rag_query(
    user_question: str,
    retrieved_chunks: list[str],
    conversation_history: list[dict],
) -> str:
    # Count fixed overhead
    question_tokens = len(enc.encode(user_question))
    history_tokens = sum(
        len(enc.encode(m["content"])) + 8  # role overhead
        for m in conversation_history
    )
    overhead = SYSTEM_TOKENS + question_tokens + history_tokens + OUTPUT_RESERVE
    available_for_context = MAX_CONTEXT - overhead

    # Greedily add chunks until budget is full
    context_parts = []
    context_tokens = 0
    for chunk in retrieved_chunks:
        n = len(enc.encode(chunk))
        if context_tokens + n > available_for_context:
            break
        context_parts.append(chunk)
        context_tokens += n

    context_str = "\n\n---\n\n".join(context_parts)
    user_content = f"Documentation:\n{context_str}\n\nQuestion: {user_question}"

    messages = (
        [{"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT}]
        + conversation_history
        + [{"role": "user", "content": user_content}]
    )

    total_input = len(enc.encode(str(messages)))
    print(f"Total input tokens: {total_input:,} / {MAX_CONTEXT:,}")
    print(f"Context chunks included: {len(context_parts)}/{len(retrieved_chunks)}")

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4-turbo",
        messages=messages,
        max_tokens=OUTPUT_RESERVE,
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

# Example call
answer = rag_query(
    user_question="How do I reset my password?",
    retrieved_chunks=["To reset your password, go to Settings > Security..."] * 20,
    conversation_history=[],
)
print("Answer:", answer[:200])

This pattern is used at scale by companies handling thousands of daily support tickets. The budget calculation ensures no call silently truncates relevant documentation, and the greedy chunk selection prioritizes the most relevant (highest-ranked) chunks within the available budget.

# WRONG: using character count as token proxy
def wrong_fits_in_context(text: str, max_chars: int = 400_000) -> bool:
    # ~4 chars/token is only a rough average
    return len(text) <= max_chars

# CORRECT: use the actual tokenizer
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

def fits_in_context(text: str, max_tokens: int = 128_000) -> bool:
    return len(enc.encode(text)) <= max_tokens

# Example: Chinese text uses ~2 chars/token, emoji uses ~3 tokens per glyph
emoji_text = "😀" * 1000
print(f"Chars: {len(emoji_text)}")
print(f"Tokens: {len(enc.encode(emoji_text))}")  # ~3000, not 250

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

# WRONG: filling context to max_tokens, leaving no room for output
def bad_call(prompt: str) -> str:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4-turbo",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        # No max_tokens set, but if prompt is 127K tokens, there's only
        # 1K tokens left for the response — typically too short
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

# CORRECT: budget for output explicitly
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

def good_call(prompt: str, output_reserve: int = 4_000) -> str:
    n_input = len(enc.encode(prompt))
    available_output = 128_000 - n_input
    if available_output < output_reserve:
        raise ValueError(f"Prompt uses {n_input} tokens, leaving only {available_output} for output")
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4-turbo",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        max_tokens=min(output_reserve, available_output),
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content
Mistake 3: Treating context truncation as an API error

# WRONG: assuming the API will raise an error on over-length input
# Most APIs silently truncate — there is NO exception

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

# This call with a too-long prompt silently truncates the beginning
try:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4",  # 8K context limit for older gpt-4
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "x" * 50_000}],  # way too long
    )
    # No exception! Response is based on truncated input.
except Exception as e:
    print("Error:", e)  # Only raises for certain models/configurations

# CORRECT: check length before calling
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
MAX_MODEL_CONTEXT = 8_192  # for gpt-4

def safe_call(prompt: str) -> str:
    n = len(enc.encode(prompt))
    if n > MAX_MODEL_CONTEXT - 500:
        raise ValueError(f"Prompt ({n} tokens) exceeds safe limit")
    ...
Always measure tokens with the model-specific tokenizer before building prompts. Encode the complete assembled prompt (not components separately) to catch unexpected token inflation from formatting.

Budget token allocation explicitly: assign a fixed budget to system prompt, conversation history, retrieved context, and output. Track consumption at each step and enforce limits before calling the API.

Place the most important instructions at the beginning and end of the context window, not in the middle. The 'lost in the middle' effect means instructions buried deep in a 128K context are often poorly attended to.

Use streaming APIs for long-context responses. Streaming reduces perceived latency by delivering partial results as they are generated, even though time-to-first-token for a 128K context is longer than for a 4K context.
Cache KV states (via prefix caching where available) for static system prompts. Many providers offer prompt caching at a discount; a 10K-token system prompt cached saves cost on every call.

For RAG, prefer many short, precise chunks over few long, diffuse chunks. A 500-token chunk that precisely answers the question is more useful than a 3,000-token chunk that buries the answer in noise.
Context window scaling is dominated by the quadratic attention bottleneck. For a model with
, the FLOP count for a single forward pass grows as:

. Flash Attention and similar IO-aware attention algorithms reduce the memory footprint to O(n) (versus O(n²) for naive attention) and improve throughput by operating on tiles that fit in SRAM, but they do not reduce the fundamental FLOP count.

Time-to-first-token (TTFT) scales roughly linearly with input length in practice, because the prefill computation is highly parallelizable across sequence positions. Generated token latency (time per output token) scales only logarithmically with context length due to KV cache lookups.

# Empirical latency estimation for different context lengths
# (based on published benchmarks for gpt-4-turbo on OpenAI's infrastructure)

# Approximate TTFT for gpt-4-turbo (in seconds) by input token count:
ttft_estimates = {
    4_000: 1.5,
    16_000: 4.0,
    32_000: 7.5,
    64_000: 14.0,
    128_000: 28.0,
}

for ctx, ttft in ttft_estimates.items():
    print(f"  {ctx:>7,} tokens → TTFT ~{ttft:.1f}s")

# Implication: for latency-sensitive applications, minimize context
# even when the model supports larger windows
OpenAI's tokenizer library. Use it to count tokens for any GPT model before sending API calls.
Chunks documents by token count using a specified tokenizer, with configurable overlap. Used in RAG preprocessing pipelines.
Splits source documents into nodes with configurable token-based size limits and overlap for indexing.
Triton-based GPU kernels that make long-context inference memory-efficient. Required for context windows beyond ~32K on consumer hardware.
Production inference engine that uses paged KV cache management, enabling efficient batching of requests with variable context lengths.

# LangChain: token-aware text splitting
from langchain.text_splitter import TokenTextSplitter

splitter = TokenTextSplitter(
    encoding_name="cl100k_base",
    chunk_size=1000,       # max tokens per chunk
    chunk_overlap=100,     # token overlap between consecutive chunks
)

long_doc = "This is a very long document. " * 500
chunks = splitter.split_text(long_doc)
print(f"Split into {len(chunks)} chunks")

import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks[:3]):
    print(f"  Chunk {i}: {len(enc.encode(chunk))} tokens")

Documents placed in the context window may contain adversarial instructions targeting the model. Retrieved web pages, user-uploaded files, or third-party tool outputs can all attempt to override system instructions. Use sandboxed prompt structures and always place system instructions last (or repeat them) to resist injection.

Most APIs silently truncate prompts that exceed the context limit, discarding the oldest content. In multi-turn agents, this can silently erase task context, leading to hallucinations. Always validate token counts pre-call.

In self-hosted deployments that share KV caches across users, a user's prior context could theoretically influence subsequent users' calls if cache partitioning is misconfigured. Ensure KV caches are session-scoped, not shared globally.

A bug that repeats content or fails to truncate history can exponentially inflate context length per turn, causing unexpected cost spikes. Monitor token usage per call and set hard limits at the application layer.

In long agentic sessions, the context window fills with earlier steps that may contradict later discoveries. Implement explicit context management that summarizes or drops stale information to keep the model focused on current state.

The context window is a whiteboard with a fixed number of squares. Every token you write on the whiteboard costs one square. The model can only reason about what is currently written — it has no memory of conversations that have been erased, documents that were never placed on the board, or intermediate results that overflowed and fell off the edge. The model is a remarkably capable reader of whatever is on the whiteboard, but it is completely blind to anything that is not.

A useful practical mental model is the 'token budget spreadsheet': before building any prompt, list each component (system prompt, history, retrieved docs, user message, output reserve) and track how many tokens each consumes. Optimize the most expensive line items first — a system prompt that was written verbosely for human readability often consumes 5-10x more tokens than a tightly written equivalent.

The lost-in-the-middle phenomenon can be understood through the attention mechanism: transformer attention is computed relative to the current query position. Tokens at the very beginning and very end of a sequence have many query positions that attend to them (the first token is attended to from every subsequent position). Tokens in the middle of a 128K sequence are attended to from fewer relative positions in the trained distribution, leading to weaker incorporation of their content into the model's output.
The context window defines the model's working memory: only tokens within the window can influence the current output, regardless of what the model has 'seen' in other sessions.
Standard self-attention scales as O(n²) in context length, making very long contexts expensive in FLOPs, memory, and API cost; quadrupling context length quadruples attention computation.
Always measure tokens with the model's tokenizer before sending API calls — character-count estimates are unreliable, especially for non-ASCII text, code, and emoji.
Reserve output tokens explicitly: filling the input budget to maximum leaves insufficient space for the model's response, causing truncated or cut-off completions.

Place critical instructions at the beginning and end of the context; the 'lost in the middle' effect means content buried in the center of a very long context is often underweighted by the model's attention.
Implement explicit token budget tracking in agentic systems: assign budgets per component, monitor consumption, and enforce limits before each API call to prevent silent truncation.
Cache static system prompts where provider prefix caching is available; a 10K-token system prompt cached saves cost on every repeated call, which is significant at scale.
